questions.question: Why is adding a secondary index never a free optimization?
options: Because indexes increase write work, storage usage, and operational overhead.
Because indexes can only be used by background jobs.
Because indexes force all queries to become strongly consistent globally.
Because indexes remove the need for primary keys.
questions.explanation: Indexes speed reads by adding cost elsewhere, especially on writes and storage.

questions.question: What question should come before deciding to add an index?
options: Which query deserves to be cheap often enough to justify permanent maintenance cost?
Which index name sounds most intuitive to the team?
How many columns can fit in the index definition syntax?
Whether the product might become multi-region one day.
questions.explanation: Indexes exist to serve important queries, so their value must justify their cost.

questions.question: What is write amplification in the context of indexing?
options: The tendency for reads to fan out across many replicas.
The extra storage updates and maintenance work caused by each logical write.
The number of clients that can retry the same request.
The increase in response payload size after pagination.
questions.explanation: A single logical write may update the base row, multiple indexes, and replicated logs.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that index count is hurting the write path?
options: Write latency rising while logical write volume stays similar.
Average UI session duration increasing.
Only total read QPS across the fleet.
Percentage of successful TLS handshakes.
questions.explanation: Stable traffic with worse commit latency strongly suggests write-path cost increased.

questions.question: When is a derived search system often better than another compound secondary index?
options: When the query surface becomes flexible and hard to bound in the OLTP store.
When there is only one stable lookup by primary key.
When the workload has no write traffic at all.
When the table is smaller than expected.
questions.explanation: Flexible search often belongs in a system built for that query style rather than many OLTP indexes.

questions.question: What is the safest way to think about index backfills in interviews?
options: They are instant and do not affect production capacity planning.
They should be throttled, measured, and validated because they compete with serving resources.
They are only a concern for on-premise databases.
They remove the need for replicas during rollout.
questions.explanation: Backfills consume I/O and compute, so they need rollout planning like any other heavy job.

questions.question: What is the key trade-off of a covering index?
options: Fewer reads may touch the base table, but storage and write maintenance cost increase.
It guarantees all writes become serializable.
It prevents any secondary query from going stale.
It lets the system avoid choosing a primary key.
questions.explanation: Covering indexes can speed reads significantly, but you pay for the duplicated indexed data.

questions.question: Which interview answer sounds most senior?
options: I will add indexes later once the table gets large enough.
I will add indexes for all current filters so no stakeholder is blocked.
I will support the top query paths directly and explicitly reject or offload the rest.
I will use a NoSQL store so indexes are not needed.
questions.explanation: Strong answers optimize intentionally and refuse unsupported query shapes instead of pretending everything is cheap.
